draft.introduction: Chapter 13 distinguishes the field (kṣetra, the body) from the knower of the field (kṣetrajña, the Self). True knowledge is knowing the difference between them.

translation.text: Arjuna said: Prakriti and Purusha, the field and the knower of the field, knowledge and that which is to be known—these I wish to learn. The Blessed Lord said: This body is called the field, and he who knows it is called the knower of the field.
translation.original_text: arjuna uvāca | prakṛtiṃ puruṣaṃ caiva kṣetraṃ kṣetrajñam eva ca

translation.text: He who sees the supreme Lord dwelling alike in all beings, the imperishable in the perishable—he truly sees. For seeing the Lord equally present everywhere, he does not injure the Self by the self.

translator_notes.note: The kṣetra/kṣetrajña distinction parallels Sāṅkhya’s prakr̥ti/puruṣa categories. But the Gita adds a third element absent from classical Sāṅkhya: the supreme Lord (parameśvara) who transcends both field and knower.
